summary: The author developed a multi-stage pipeline for ECG image digitization, beginning with orientation correction, followed by heatmap-based keypoint estimation and iterative registration, piecewise homography-based lead cropping, and dual-encoder UNet heatmap prediction to extract lead waveforms. A core insight was prioritizing high input resolution over model complexity to preserve fine-grained details, alongside using a dual-encoder architecture that combines a high-resolution VGG19 branch with a global encoder (CoaT or ConvNeXT) to capture both local texture and long-range dependencies. The pipeline leverages iterative pseudo-labeling for keypoint refinement, column-wise Jensen-Shannon Divergence loss for heatmap decoding, and a blank template concatenation to simplify grid alignment learning. Final results were achieved through strategic model ensembling, lead fusion, and a dedicated refinement stage for out-of-range predictions, securing a 5th place finish.
extensive_summary:
## Pipeline Overview & Keypoint Estimation
The solution follows a strict multi-stage workflow: orientation correction → heatmap-based keypoint estimation → keypoint registration → lead cropping → heatmap-based lead waveform estimation. For keypoint detection, a 2D UNet predicts a 58-channel heatmap (1 grid channel + 57 main feature-rich channels). Initial pseudo-labels are generated using the dense matching model MINIMA-RoMa, then refined through iterative pseudo-labeling across 5 folds to denoise predictions. The model uses a multi-task loss strategy: BCE for the multimodal grid channel and Channel-Masked JSD for the unimodal main keypoint channels, weighted via GLS. Sub-pixel accuracy is further improved by finetuning with an MSELoss on DSNT predictions. Heavy augmentations (Affine, Perspective, RandomCrop, Dropout variants, etc.) and a longest-resize + padding strategy are applied, with resolution prioritized over model depth.

## Keypoint Registration & Lead Cropping
Heatmaps are decoded via argmax for main keypoints and NMS + Hungarian matching for grid keypoints. A global Homography matrix is estimated via RANSAC, followed by an iterative local Homography interpolation loop to handle non-planar distortion and missing detections. For lead cropping, the author compares global vs. piecewise homography approaches, ultimately selecting a piecewise method using K=16 nearby keypoints per cell to balance locality and robustness against grid estimation errors. Warping/resampling consistently uses `cv2.INTER_LANCZOS4` to preserve fine details.

## Waveform Estimation & Modeling
The final stage predicts lead waveforms using another UNet that outputs column-wise 1D Gaussian heatmaps. The encoding scheme uses an adaptive sigma scale based on local waveform variance to handle sharp peaks. The architecture employs a dual-encoder design: a VGG19 branch extracts fine-grained features at strides 1/2/4, while a CoaT Lite Medium or ConvNeXT Large branch aggregates global context. Each lead crop is concatenated with a grayscale blank template (rendered via ecg-image-kit) to form a 4-channel input, helping the model learn local grid alignment. Column-wise JSD loss is used, and high resolution (1024x1024) is proven critical for SNR.

## Final Submission & Key Insights
The submission combines a weighted ensemble (0.7/0.3) of the two dual-encoder models, fuses the first quarter of Lead II (0.5/0.5), and applies a separate "TALL" model as a refinement stage for predictions near the dynamic range limits. Key technical takeaways include: resolution matters more than model size; column-wise JSD outperforms BCE/CE for unimodal heatmaps; dual-encoders effectively split local/global feature extraction; and blank templates reduce learning complexity for grid alignment.
competition_flow: Raw ECG images are orientation-corrected, processed through a multi-stage pipeline involving heatmap-based keypoint detection and iterative registration, piecewise homography-based lead cropping, and dual-encoder UNet heatmap prediction to extract lead waveforms, followed by ensemble fusion and range refinement for final submission.
data_processing: Longest resize + padding with bicubic interpolation
Heavy augmentations: Affine, Perspective, RandomCrop, Grayscale, RandomBrightnessContrast, ColorJitter, Downscale, Blur, Noise, Dropout (Coarse, Grid, XYMasking)
Concatenation of grayscale blank template to RGB lead crops to form 4-channel inputs
Addition of truncated normal noise (σ=0.4px) to detected grid keypoints before cropping
Lanczos4 interpolation for warping and resampling
Adaptive Gaussian sigma scaling based on local waveform variance
cv_strategy: 5-fold cross-validation with Out-Of-Fold (OOF) predictions used for iterative pseudo-labeling and model selection.
ensembling: Final submission combines a weighted ensemble (0.7/0.3) of two dual-encoder models, fuses the first quarter of Lead II (0.5/0.5 weights), and applies a refinement stage using a separate "TALL" model to correct predictions near the dynamic range limits.
insights: Prioritizing input image resolution over model complexity is critical for preserving fine-grained details and reducing sub-pixel error.
Column-wise JSD loss provides better inductive bias and faster convergence than BCE or CE for unimodal heatmap distributions.
Using a dual-encoder architecture (high-resolution VGG19 + global CoaT/ConvNeXT) effectively captures both local texture details and long-range dependencies.
Concatenating a grayscale blank template with lead crops helps the model learn local grid alignment and reduces learning complexity.
Adaptive Gaussian sigma scaling based on local waveform variance improves prediction accuracy for sharp peaks and high-gradient regions.
critical_findings: Scaling the image size during inference without retraining causes severe SNR degradation, as the model fails to generalize to new input dimensions.
Ignoring near-border grid keypoints during training simplified the task but caused significant registration headaches later, suggesting all keypoints should be retained.
Deformable Convolution showed performance gains primarily due to increased parameter count rather than actual deformability, and was discarded for slower runtime.

Training on a smaller input size (512x512) and inferring on a larger size (1024x512) to handle wider waveform ranges resulted in very bad SNR, highlighting the need for joint input/output resolution scaling.
what_did_not_work: PixelShuffle-based decoder did not outperform traditional F.interpolate() for upsampling.
Modifying encoder stride from 4 to 1 or 2 did not yield gains over standard stride 4 with 1024x1024 input/output.
Using a single global Homography matrix for cropping caused misalignment due to local distortion.
Standard augmentations (ColorJitter, BrightnessContrast, Grayscale, small Affine/Perspective) provided little gain and were downweighted.
Training the final dual-encoder architecture without validation before the deadline resulted in a messy submission process, though it still performed well.
